summary:
The Treaty of Nanking, signed on 29 August 1842 aboard a British warship anchored at Nanjing, ended the [[event:2541|First Opium War]] and established the template for a series of 'unequal treaties' that the Western powers and Japan would impose on China over the following decades. It was the first of these treaties and remains a central symbol of the 'century of humiliation' in Chinese national memory.

The treaty's terms were sweeping. China ceded Hong Kong island to Britain 'in perpetuity,' opening a Crown Colony that would endure until 1997. Five ports were opened to British trade and residence: Canton, Amoy, Fuzhou, Ningbo, and Shanghai. The traditional Cohong trading monopoly was abolished, opening trade to all British merchants. China agreed to pay an indemnity of 21 million silver dollars. A subsequent supplementary treaty added extraterritoriality — British subjects in China would be tried under British rather than Chinese law — and most-favored-nation status, ensuring that any concessions China granted to other powers would automatically apply to Britain.

The treaty's significance extended beyond its immediate terms. It established the concept of 'treaty ports' that would proliferate across China, creating semi-sovereign enclaves where Western commercial and religious activity operated largely outside Chinese legal jurisdiction. The model was replicated by other Western powers who demanded similar rights in subsequent years, progressively eroding Chinese sovereignty.
